sentences.text: I understand.
[SIGHS] Well, I am fortunate that I have got lovely three children.
Well, one of them, unfortunately, is retarded.
But she's still my lovely daughter.
I've got my lovely son, wonderful, loving boy.
And a very beautiful daughter who-- Each one of them gave me grandchildren, they brought me pleasure.
They enriched my life at a time I needed most enrichment.
My father has dedicated and given of himself the hardest time, the hardest year when I was small.
And as I grew up, and I became an independent person, a thinking person, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel that I was not able to say thank you, Father, for helping me to become what I am.
Without you, I would not be this because I did not have a teacher.
I did not have a guide.
It was all just using the strength that I've received from you, that I've seen you, the love that you have given to Mother, the love that you have given to family.
And that's how I learned to give this.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because I have no one else that would have taught me how to do this.
Therefore, it is you that has given this.
Yet, what have I done for you?
I feel I have been denied the pleasure of-- of saving you, helping you.
Growing up, when you get older, being-- standing by you and saying thank you for making me me.
Giving me the strength that I have.
It must be because of your genes and your mind, and your kindness and gentleness that you have.
But I have been denied all of that.
It is beyond me when I hear anybody say that I had a father and then they go into a dissertation of how terrible their father was.
I cannot relate to it.
I relate to father as a godly figure.
God and father.
And sometimes father and God.
And faith has taken that away from me, and I needed it.
And also say with-- with Mother.
But somehow, I feel more strength came from Father to me, and more strength came to the girls from Mother.
Each one gets the strength differently.
In my mind, Father has come into my dreams for years.
Mother, less.
So I feel that yes, God, I believe in you.
But you have certainly taken a lot away from me.
One of them was the pleasure of saying thank you, Dad.
I'm here, I'll stand by you, I'll walk you through.
Yes.
Also for myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I survived for myself.
I have-- he is not, and I'm a realist.
He doesn't know that I've survived.
Yes, spiritually.
Spiritually, we should draw strength from that.
That I have fulfilled this wish because if he were asked, what is your biggest wish, losing a son or dying?
He would have said saving a son.
Take me, God, but save my sons.
Or save my daughters.
I know that spiritually.
But while I'm a spiritualist, I'm also a realist.
He doesn't know.
I do not believe in after death.
After death is only-- you become immortal if you're remembered what-- by what you do on this earth, and you improve the life of people-- especially children-- on this earth.
And you're remembered by that.
Or you invent a cure for a disease, you become immortal.

If you do nothing but create [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] money, business, and-- and-- and you enjoy life, and you die but you have not been creative in improving the future of any individual or people-- you are not immortal.
You're gone.
Nothing afterwards.
So I do not believe that because-- I'm referring back, and I'm not trying to confuse it.
But I do not believe that it's sufficient your statement that I did something for my father, because he had not had a chance to live long enough to see what I have turned into being.
I was just a kid.
And I have not had a chance.

God was good to me, he gave me strength, he gave me health, he gave me the opportunity to recuperate, bring grandchildren-- children and grandchildren-- and never gave him the pleasure of enjoying them.
[SIGHS] You have to be a strong spiritualist and believe in that.
To a degree, yes.
If I want to justify it, yes.
But a pain occasionally is still there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I am because he lived.
But I would not be here.
He gave me birth.
So I am.
But the pain is that he didn't do it for me.
I didn't do anything for him.
Precisely.
And more painful.
I would try to shout out and hug him.
I'd say, thank you, Dad, for making me be what I am so I could have the strength to survive, recover, and make something out of myself.
But how do you-- who do I hug?
Who do I thank?
He doesn't have a grave.
I cannot go to a gravesite and talk to him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So he's just a spirit someplace, if you believe in after death.
At least if he died, go to the gravesite and it's a release.
He doesn't have a gravesite.
So his gravesite is every memorial that's being built to me, I try to put his name on.
And I say Kaddish there.
So-- because there is no specific place.
Do you know where he perished?
No.
I've been trying to search most of my life and heard rumors.
I do not know specific.
I know some other family members, not him.
The latest rumor that I heard-- which, I must say, it's rumored, it's-- it's not been proven-- that he was-- they were marching them into a camp, and because he was in a [? medicorps-- ?]
No.
One of the Polish camps.
And that there were over 200 of them, and they decided they don't want these people to-- to be witness and to be alive, because they know too much.
They know too much what they did with the sick and so forth, that they shot them against the mountainside.
Someone after the war immediately told me, he has seen my father dead.
And that he was shot with a group of others that were doing the same type of work as he did.
This is, again, a rumor.
I don't know how true this is.
I have written several times to Arolsen, which is an international tracing service in Germany.
I've written three different occasions, I cannot get an answer.
I'm expecting, again, an answer.
So I am searching for that.
It was after the liberation, someone that was with him.
But I do not remember the name.
It was vague.
It was very vague, what it was.
Someone in distant relations.
From-- I'm trying very hard to remember.
It was some sort of a distant relation, because he came from an area called Khust, which was a very strong Jewish community, a large Jewish community.
And people were traveling everywhere, scattered, trying to get into Palestine, they couldn't.
Trying to get into Canada, they couldn't.
Trying to get everywhere, so we wound up in displaced person camps.
And on one occasion, someone said, I've seen your dad.
But this-- I-- this was not a definite identification.
The way he said it, it was with a bunch of people and they were all shot.
And it was an older person.
I remember that, I don't remember his name.
And it may just have been to put my mind at ease.
Don't search for him.
He's dead.
I never knew the real reason.
I never knew the truth, or where he was killed, or how he died.
Yes, oh yes.
Because I do remember.
It was an older person, I was a youngster, and-- and he just wanted to say go on, go on.
Join a kibbutz.
Go to Israel.
Your dad is gone.
Oh, definitely.
Definitely an act of kindness.
Why?
Because we were always hoping and searching, and hoping and searching.
And while there were many, many people that did find family, I knew that if four-- three, four months passed by and no one else came home, that they would not be alive.
Because we had relatives in Budapest, and everyone returned from the camp through Budapest or Bucharest.
And the Budapest family never saw him, never heard of him.
He was a very intelligent man, and he would not have made the mistake of not showing up in Budapest.
Since he didn't show up in Budapest, I didn't have any hopes that he came back.
But it was a different time, a different era.
People got lost, confused.
He may have confused him with another Basch.
Mordechai ?] Basch.
There were several Basches.
It's not a common name, but there were several Basches.
Well, these are the Basch children.
Well, I saw Basch being killed.
It may have been my father, it may have been anyone else.
But, you know, it's hard to have to rationalize because, at that time, there was no rational reason behind anything.
It was wild.
It was-- We were like loose-- loose little animals searching to find a place, to go somewhere.
To-- to-- and rumors flew all over the place.
Oh, I saw your brother, [? so. ?] Sometimes it was true, sometimes they didn't exist, sometimes it was wishful thinking, sometimes there was a confusion of names.
So I didn't take it seriously.
I knew that he's dead, and I didn't question it at that time.
Because remember, we also struggled to reestablish ourselves and to-- to know where are we going to go, what are we going to do.
And it was not that-- it was not the most important issue.
He was gone, it was that.
It was not as important, then, to me as a child, as it's important now as I grew older.
Because I did not have the appreciation for what he was at the age of 17 and 18. 18 when I was liberated.
I saw it with different eyes, through a boy's eyes.
And I-- later on, I saw it through a man's eyes.
Main thing, we were-- we were strong.
We were strong.
It's the survival.
Remember, the-- the main question is asked, how did you survive camp?
The will to live is probably the closest answer.
Because if you didn't have that, you died.
And then luck.
Just lucky breaks.
I mean, there were a thousand reasons why I should not survived.
And eventually, as we get into it deeper, I hopefully have a chance to explain that I came near to be shot, killed, over and over and over again.
So that was luck.
However, you also had to want to live.
Therefore, you sometimes created that luck, intentionally or unintentionally.
But if you gave up and you didn't believe that you'll make it, you died, because you did not put yourself in a position where luck will play your directions.
And I don't want to jump ahead, but as we get in later, I will explain some of the things that I did that may sound cruel, but it was survival.
It wasn't as painful as it is painful now to me to think back.
I was a kid.
I was 14 years old, 15 years old.
My-- my mother took me to Budapest.
It was in 1940-- late '42.
No.
I remember fall.
And she found someone who was willing to take me in and to teach me tailoring.
But remember, I was an apprentice, and I did not get any pay, any food, except lodging on a cutting table.
That was it, that was my payment.
Therefore, we went around-- being my uncle lived in Budapest, he had some acquaintances.
We went around from acquaintance to acquaintance, from-- we had some distant cousins, distant families, and asked each one to give me one day.
Food for a day.
On Monday, one family.
Tuesday, another family.
Wednesday, a third family.
And we found seven families that pledged to my mother that he can come and eat.
After we are finished, what's left over, he can come after lunch, after dinner, and we'll give him that food.
So I survived that way for a period of time, several months.
I slept on a cutting table, I learned some of tailoring, and ate-- it was called-- [? tagas. ?] It's-- it's daily a different family gave me the food.
Were these gentile families?
No, mostly Jewish families.
No gentile family.
A gentile family wouldn't have anything to do with us.
So that--
Jews and gentiles didn't mix that freely.
Even in the big cities.
Only if you were an upper echelon, a wealthy one.
If you were a poor kid from the Carpathian, they wouldn't have anything to do with you.
You were just a kid from the Carpathian area, from-- it didn't matter.
They wouldn't bother, they wouldn't even open the door.
So that's how I survived at the beginning.
And then in Budapest, I got acquainted with-- with-- with more and more people, and things started getting worse in Budapest, too.
And I had to join forced labor camp because as a Jew, I was not allowed to be free.
I was allowed to go home to sleep, but only if I give my time to the government.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's from being in fairly free, we started becoming less free.
And that pressure started getting worse and worse and worse.
And we survived.
We survived as best as we could.
Till really, when it got bad, when Horthy resigned and the Arrow Cross took over.
Then Jews were immediately panicking.
Young Jews were grabbed up and carried out of the city, taken out of the city.
[INAUDIBLE]
Yes, that was already in 1944, in 1944.
And survival was working for contractors that worked for the government.
The contractor got paid, but we were the free labor to the contractor.
But we were allowed to go home at night, come back in the morning.
And if we didn't, there was a death warrant out for us.
No, no, no.
It was very difficult to communicate with the family.
And my family was taken away in 1944 in April.
They were taken away to Auschwitz.
An interesting story, when my family was gathered up and taken to a larger town from where they were deported-- And--
Sevlush. ?] It was-- the city was [?
Sevlush. ?] It was a brickyard, and that's where my father, mother, and two sisters were taken.
They were in ghetto, and then they were deported, all of them, to Auschwitz.
And the first time that I was aware that they are not at home anymore is when I received a postcard signed by my mother.
Dear son, we have been repatriated by the German government.
We live on a farm.
We are doing farm work now, but we are together, and we are OK.
So don't worry, and so on and so forth, signed in her handwriting.

What the Germans did is-- evidently, for some political reasons, they didn't want to spread the name of Auschwitz, especially to the Jews in Budapest at that time, [? yet. ?] And then they asked the parents or-- my mother, they asked her, do you have any kids?
And she gave the addresses of the kids.
So she addressed a pre-printed postcard which stated that they had been repatriated all the time to confuse and all the time not to let the reality out.
And mother signed that postcard.
So all she had on that is addressed in her own handwriting and signature in her own handwriting, but it was a pre-printed postcard.
And while I didn't believe it, I wanted to believe it, that they are together and they are alive.
Yes.
We knew-- we knew, and we doubted it.
But, you know, it's amazing, it's amazing how the desire to believe the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] impossible, sometimes, because it's so terrible to believe the truth.
We sometimes tend to believe the lies.
Oh, we-- we heard it already, while I was in Budapest.
We already heard it because a lot of Polish kids that escaped from Poland, many of them escaped from the Warsaw ghetto, too.
Where could they have gone?
They couldn't get to Russia, because that was the war front.
And we did-- by that time, I joined, already, an underground, and we didn't whatever we could to assist them.
Because I spoke the language, I was at home, they were not.
So we had-- we did everything we could to protect them and get them out of Budapest to Romania, and from Romania there was another organization that took over.
And the Jewish organizations, the Jewish movement, has leased boats and-- through the Black Sea, they try to get in through-- through-- Iran-- through not Iran, but where the Black Sea borders.
Bulgaria, but then it borders-- the--
Turkey, through Turkey and tried to get into Palestine that way.
Unfortunately, out of seven ships that I was instrumental-- or not singlehandedly-- but with the group that we were instrumental, out of seven, only two of them made it.
From what we have heard, that three of them sank, two of them were torpedoed by the English, and two of them made it through.
I was supposed to get on the last ship because I was working with this organization.
It was called [?
Dorhapunames ?] [?
Anis ?] organization, that organized this.
And I-- they never wanted me to get on because I was fluent in Hungarian, I knew my way around, and I was able to deliver false certificates, gentile papers, to these people.
Which now there were not that many that were able to do that.
And they always asked me to stay on, to stay on, to stay on a little longer, and I never got on any of the boats.
And then it was too late and it was cut off, because by that time, Horthy resigned, the Arrow Cross took over, Eichmann came into Budapest.
And that-- that's when it started, the year where Raoul Wallenberg came to Budapest.
And we changed our policy and we started working through Raoul Wallenberg.
Spent my nights in a printing shop.
We started before Wallenberg, we really became strong when Wallenberg was there.
Because Wallenberg couldn't have done what he did by himself.
He had hundreds and hundreds of helpers.
But mostly they were young kids like me, 15, 16 years old.
We knew the city and our biggest job was if we got a hold of six passes-- six Schutz-Pass, Schutz-- what's that thing they call it.

That Raoul Wallenberg's office issued, we copied it, we duplicated it, it looked very authentic, and we were able to give this out and-- and-- and distributed it to people that were not able to get it from the Wallenberg organization directly.
I'm sure you've noticed already from history that he has-- when he came to Budapest, the first thing he did is there were some wealthy Jews that donated a lot of money for him and their homes.
Not their homes, their houses.
They were sort of courtyards, and some consisted of 30, 40 apartments.
They were large hotel buildings where Jews were beginning to move in to ghetto and they were empty, so Raoul Wallenberg bought them from the German government for very little.
And he put up the Swedish flag, and he housed these people in there.
Well, it was one thing to get them in there.
But to get them out there and to be able to get them out of the country, that was difficult.
Raoul Wallenberg himself couldn't deliver all Schutz-Pass or the-- or the passports himself.
So he used people like us.
We not only duplicated many of them, but we also delivered this during the night.
And most of my-- most of the time, we used the sewer system at night.
Because every-- in those countries, in Poland, they have the courtyard and then they've got this huge sewer outlet in the center of the courtyard.
We got into the sewer system, and I knew my way how to get into the courtyard.
And once I got there, I brought food and passports and whatnot.
And then we were able to legitimately get them into another group that has snuck them through the border into Romania and, again, try to get them.
What happened to many of them, we don't know.
But the idea was to get them out of Budapest.
Mostly, mostly the Polish younger people that have escaped.
Those were the first ones that we were concerned with, because they didn't speak English.
I mean, sorry, they didn't speak Hungarian, and it was very, very difficult for them to move around.
So that's what I did for quite--
